data.title: Why Markets And The Economy In 2026 May End Up Looking A Lot Like 2025
data.description:
With 2026 underway, there is a lot for investors to navigate. TD Cowen has come out with its Best Ideas 2026 report, and among the key takeaways - this year is likely going to look a lot like last year. Lisa Thomas, Managing Director, Deputy Head of Global Research at TD Cowen, discusses the main macro themes to watch.

Transcript

Kim Parlee: With 2026 underway, there's a lot for investors to navigate through. But TD Cowen has come out with its "Best Ideas of 2026," and one of the key takeaways is that this year is going to look a lot like last year.

So joining us now to break down the big themes and also some stocks to watch, Lisa Thomas of TD Cowen joins us now. Lisa, nice to see you.

Lisa Thomas: Great to see you. Happy New Year.

Kim Parlee: Happy New Year to you too. Why do you think 2026 looks more like a continuation of 2025, and that something different is not going to happen?

Lisa Thomas: Sure. Certainly already in the first couple of weeks of the year, we and markets are digesting a lot in terms of news flow. But when you do break it down and spend time with our Washington policy analysts, I think that there really is a clear continuation of the America First agenda that President Trump and his administration had started to implement back in January of last year.

I think we see that on the geopolitical front, as well as on the domestic side. And so, again, it feels like a lot to react to, but it's very consistent in terms of the direction.

Kim Parlee: I think a lot of people were quite surprised by the resilience of many economies last year. And I think you see that continuing. You see from the numbers that you put in your report, the global economy is going to continue to grow. The US economy will continue to outperform. So where is the resilience coming from?

Lisa Thomas: That's right. Part of the resilience comes from productivity that is really a realization of productivity from workers who were hired coming out of the end of COVID. So, as you recall, there was a tremendous amount of hiring during that period. And it takes a while for that to really realize at a whole economic level. I think that we're seeing that now, and that's part of the gain.

But another thing that I'd call out is fiscal policy. The president passed last year in the US the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. And with that, we are expecting a tremendous uptick in federal tax refunds for US consumers and households this year.

Those refunds at a household level could go up by 40-45%. It's about $150 billion in the US projected. So those kinds of things, that addition to worker productivity, and then household spending being fueled as well by tax refunds. Both of those things are going to help keep the economy moving.

Kim Parlee: I certainly would like a 40-45% increase in my tax refund, but I don't think that's happening. But anyway, let's talk about Canada. And when you look at the Canadian outlook, it's powered by something different and you're focused a lot more on the fiscal policy side of things.

Lisa Thomas: That's right. I mean, that's really a function of budget increases that for this year are 7% versus last, '26 over '25. That's going to make a big difference. That's $30 billion for this year, which alone could add 80 basis points-- nearly a full percentage of GDP just from that increase in the budget.

And that's not something that is going to be standing alone for a year. There's a five-year projection that is going to add just under $90 billion to the economy in Canada.

Kim Parlee: What are the risks? And if you wouldn't mind, I wouldn't mind talking about Canada first and then we can talk about the States. Obviously, risk to the States will also be risk to Canada. But it's an interesting time. So, Canada.

Lisa Thomas: That's right. Certainly trade remains a wild card. I think that we believe that net net, that the increases to spending through the fiscal policy will outweigh any drag caused by trade. We would flag that. You've still got about 90% of all trade is USMCA-compliant under exemptions.

And when we talk to our Canadian strategists, they're very eager to point to the fact that the continuation of those exemptions, the fact that they are in there, is a signal that should be read as saying that the US administration does want to see those continue to be in place.

So, it's entirely possible that the USMCA that we land with is going to look a lot like the last one, and that would be a benefit.

Kim Parlee: That really, really would be a benefit. What about the US? I know that you highlight in the report execution risk. And maybe just tell me a bit about what specifically you mean by that.

Lisa Thomas: Sure thing. There's certainly a lot of expectation that rests on the deregulation part of the president's agenda in the US. And where we see execution risk actually comes out in some of the news flow, in fact, that we've seen this week with the discussion around Chairman Powell and the investigation-- the DOJ investigation into the Fed and Chairman Powell that's currently in discussion.

The expectation had been that Chair Powell would step down from the Fed when his chairman term expires this May. But I think if he believes that there's an ongoing threat to Fed independence, it's certainly possible that he might remain as a Fed governor.

And that could actually make the picture more complicated when you're thinking about the Fed rate cuts that the market is expecting for this year.

Kim Parlee: Yeah, it really does-- the opposite impact, perhaps, of what the president was expecting. Can I ask you a bit about longer-term trends, too? I mean, we always want to know, it's convenient to talk about 2026 and to hear what's coming up, but I know you've got your eye on some longer-term trends as well. And you're highlighting demographics. So maybe tell us a bit about that.

Lisa Thomas: Sure. We're highlighting demographics again this year in our TD Cowen 2026 themes book, which is really the piece in which we take a big step back, open the aperture and look at those longer-term trends. When we were talking about some of the points earlier, those are really things that are subject to change, they're policy-driven, and sometimes people can be whipsawed.

But when you're thinking about things like a population, an aging population, that's something that we can see into the future and know is happening. So, for example, the aging population is something we have to pay a lot of attention to.

This past year, 2025, was the peak year for 18-year-olds in the US. From 2025 on, that number is going to decline. So you're going to have almost 2.5 million fewer 18-year-olds in less than a decade. In less than a decade, the death rate in the US will actually exceed the birth rate.

So we're really starting to think about investments and investment strategies that take account of that aging population, and the fact that you're going to have fewer younger people, younger workers, and then also can address the needs of an aging population, 65-plus. Because that population, 65-plus, is going to be 23% of the US population by 2050.

Kim Parlee: You were talking about demographics, and my probably less precise summary of it is just, everyone's aging. Everyone's getting older and we're not replenishing.

Lisa Thomas: With every day.

Kim Parlee: Yeah. So, the first idea you have from "Best Ideas 2026 Report" that you put out-- and I should mention you're putting out quite a few, we're going to talk to just a few of them-- is Ionis Pharmaceuticals (IONS).

Lisa Thomas: That's right. Ionis Pharmaceuticals is the Best Idea from our biotechnology analyst, Yaron Werber. And I think that biopharma innovation is the natural place to go when you're thinking about an aging population.

So we like Ionis because it has a number of data catalysts coming up this year. Based on our checks with physicians and experts in the field, we think that the likelihood of success in a number of those is better than understood by investors.

And they do, in particular, have some indications that relate to things like cardiomyopathy, which is a problem that is faced by a large number of people in the 60-75 population-- so a large market, again, related to aging.

And they also are working on some indications in partnership with another pharma company, that would address proteins that are relevant to early onset Alzheimer's.

Kim Parlee: Alright. And I'll just mention to people, as you say in the report, I think it's the ticker on this one, IONS. And then we also have current price around $81, price target around $99. And a reminder to everybody, do your own research. It has to make sense for whatever you're doing.

OK, the second one in terms of Best Ideas for 2026, Royalty Pharmaceuticals (RPRX).

Lisa Thomas: Yes. Royalty Pharmaceuticals is an interesting one. That's another Best Idea that's been advanced by our pharma analyst, Michael Nedelcovych. And Mike likes the stock because Royalty Pharma gives you exposure to biopharma innovation, but without that risk of binary outcomes in a clinical trial, and without the operational risk around running a pharma business.

So Royalty Pharma purchases, Royalty revenue stream rights, they can build a portfolio across markets and indications, including indications relevant to aging population. And they also are better able to manage patent risk than an actual pharma operating company.

So your return profile is going to be still projected to be 10% compounded over the next five years, according to our analyst projections. But that's with a lot of risk managed to the downside given the different structure versus owning directly in pharma.

Kim Parlee: Interesting. I think the streams of royalties is something that's familiar to a lot of Canadians through mining.

Lisa Thomas: That's right.

Kim Parlee: This time, though, through pharma--

Lisa Thomas: That's right.

Kim Parlee: --which is interesting. And again, just the ticker's on the screen for people to see it. Current trading in around $38.39 and price target of $45. Again, do your own research and make sure it makes sense for you.

OK, Apollo Global Management (APO) is your third one we're going to talk about today.

Lisa Thomas: Thank you. Yeah, I think Apollo Global Management is a really important stock to think about when you're thinking about an aging population and growth of that retiree population, bearing in mind that in the US you've got 12,000 people every day who are turning age 65.

And for any survey that's run on a retiree population, consistently, at least a third of those respondents will tell you that running out of money in retirement is a chief concern. So our analyst, Bill Katz, likes Apollo because they're particularly diverse in offering a range of guaranteed income and annuity products that are particularly well-suited for that population.

And I would say they also have upside as an alternative asset manager, because they also give exposure to some of that democratization of wealth that we're seeing, where you have things like private credit and other vehicles that used to just be available to institutional investors, can now go into retiree account investing as well.

Kim Parlee: Alright. And I want to squeeze one in really fast. A name familiar to everybody, unless you've been hiding under a rock, Amazon (AMZN).

Lisa Thomas: That's absolutely right. Amazon is a stock that our internet analyst, John Blackledge, is advancing as his Best Idea this year, in large part because of the AWS business segment, which is, of course, the backbone of AI infrastructure.

Since 2022, AWS has doubled its capacity, and they expect to be able to do that again by 2027. They have found that the demand for AI infrastructure capacity continues to be insatiable. And so, that, by our forecasts over the last 12 months, is roughly a $90 billion business-- just that segment alone.

I think that that's something that we need to think about around an aging population, because it's not just that we're building the infrastructure for its own sake-- that has to support need. And when you're thinking about mobility needs for an elderly population that's increasingly going to rely on automation.

And similarly, if you have fewer younger workers, more and more automation solutions are going to be needed to power output and productivity. And that is going to be very reliant on AI.

Kim Parlee: Alright. And just to mention again to everybody, current price, say $220, high $220s, I'll say that. Could be beyond that now. And price target of $300. But again, do your own research.

Lisa, it's always great to have you with us. Appreciate you bringing this to us.

Lisa Thomas: Likewise. Thanks so much. Take care now.

Kim Parlee: Yeah, you, too. That is Lisa Thomas of TD Cowen joining us tonight. And a reminder, as I've been saying all along, please do your own research. Make sure it makes sense for you.

data.title: Akamai Technologies: Growing Edge Opportunities In The Agentic AI Era
data.description:
Akamai Technologies, Inc. (AKAM), a pioneer in the Content Delivery Network (CDN), is a stock I have been following for years. During my first coverage of the stock in July 2020, the prevailing trend was the massive COVID-19-related surge in global internet traffic that ended up accelerating demand for AKAM's cloud security and CDN services.

Suffice it to say, the stock has not performed well since my first coverage, which may have to do with AKAM's continuous transformation into a cloud edge inference and security player, followed by the consistent decline in its legacy CDN business.

In the past five years, in fact, AKAM has been trading sideways and has been down over -12%. Nonetheless, the stock has gained some renewed momentum YTD as it trades around $93 per share as of late, possibly driven by the recent expansion into the AI inference cloud market.

I maintain my buy rating. My 1-year price target of $108.2 presents a 16% upside from the current price of $93.5 per share.

Financial Reviews

AKAM still appears to be in a transition into a newer, yet specialized edge inference cloud and security business.

Q3 revenue was $1.06 billion, a 5% YoY growth, driven primarily by two business segments. CIS (Cloud Infrastructure Services) saw a 39% YoY revenue growth to $81 million, while security revenue grew 10% YoY to $568 million. Both segments' growth offset the declining legacy CDN business, which fell 4% YoY to $306 million.

Meanwhile, profitability grew stronger in Q3, driven by operational efficiencies as well as the growth of higher-margin CIS business. In addition to Non-GAAP EPS growing by 17% YoY to $1.86, non-GAAP operating margins also saw a 31% improvement.

Liquidity and balance sheet remain solid. While the debt-to-equity ratio inched higher, at 1.1x, it is still relatively at a healthy level. AKAM ended Q3 with $1.8 billion of cash and short-term investments, with a significant contribution coming from its cash flow from operations (OCF), which was over $1.1 billion in the nine months ending Q3, similar to the same level last year.

I believe that the strong OCF, as well as the balance sheet, provide AKAM with enough dry powder to heavily invest in CAPEX to drive its new business. In that sense, AKAM is on track to achieve its target of investing in CAPEX for CIS infrastructure at 20% of its revenue target for the FY as per its earlier guidance. In the end, around $800 million was also spent in the first nine months of 2025 for share repurchases.

Catalyst

I see a few catalysts that could help AKAM maintain its uptrend share performance YTD as it continues its shift from a CDN to a cloud and security player. Given the declining legacy CDN business, these catalysts are primarily the growing trends around AI that may positively affect its CIS and Cloud Security business.

First off, I expect AKAM's edge AI inference technology to see growing demand due to many real-time use cases in enterprise AI that require better latency. Essentially, I would imagine many companies deploying agentic AI models into their operations across functions, demanding better connectivity.

As per the report by Gartner, 40% of enterprise applications will leverage AI agents in 2026, a 5% increase YoY. As such, while the cloud market appears to be saturated with players like Amazon AWS and Microsoft Azure, the AI inference cloud seems to be an underrated opportunity in 2026.

Therefore, in my opinion, while cloud security, AKAM's largest business today, continues to benefit from the increasing AI-driven cyber threats and grow in the low teens, AKAM's CIS business may continue to see sustained high growth around the 30% range, driving valuation re-rating.

Furthermore, the fact that AKAM only saw a -4% decline in the legacy CDN segment in Q3, which is a lower sequential decline compared to the previous two quarters, suggests that the CDN decline may be close to bottoming out. As a result, if the double-digit growth across CIS and Cloud Security is maintained throughout 2026, AKAM could soon be seeing less negative impact from the CDN decline, driving guidance beats.

Risk

Overall, risk seems minimal. While the 2026 outlook appears interesting, for instance, my view is that there is a likelihood that the market for edge cloud AI inference is developing more slowly than expected in 2026, since, despite the real AI trend, the enterprise agentic AI use cases are, for the most part, still in early stages. In that sense, companies would probably choose to deploy their inference AI inside their centralized hyperscaler servers, such as AWS or Azure, despite the latency benefits of edge cloud AI.

In the worst-case scenario, given the continued decline of CDN, the relatively high CAPEX investment AKAM has made to scale its CIS business, and the high-growth rate of the new CIS business that has been supporting the bullish case for re-rating, AKAM may struggle to earn its market premium into 2026.

Valuation/Pricing

My target price for AKAM is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs bear scenarios of the FY 2026 projection based on the consensus estimate:

  1. Bull scenario (60% probability) assumptions—AKAM sees CIS growth accelerating to more than 40% YoY as more enterprises are adopting AKAM's edge AI approach to reduce latency, while the legacy CDN decline continues to slow down. I give AKAM a forward P/S of 4x, at the higher end of its historical trading range, suggesting that it should transition more into being valued as a cloud AI or security instead of a commodity CDN player.

  2. Bear scenario (40% probability) assumptions—AKAM sees CIS growth stagnant, with the heavy CAPEX investment that has been around 20% of revenue failing to yield the desired ROI. More enterprises choose centralized clouds instead of the edge to deploy AI models. I assign AKAM a forward P/S of 2.7x, not at the lowest end of its valuation range, but also lower than where it is today, suggesting that any hiccups in the new business means that it could more or less still be considered a commodity CDN competitor.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $108.2 per share, presenting a potential upside of 16% from the current level. At this point, I would give the stock a buy rating.

Conclusion

AKAM enters 2026 with an attractive market narrative, successfully transforming itself from a commodity CDN competitor into a fast-growing cloud security and AI edge inference player. With the two newer businesses thriving in the growing market due to the enterprise AI trend, I have a strong belief that AKAM could potentially see a re-rating in 2026. Despite the risk of hyperscalers taking the share of the AI edge market by offering a centralized alternative to enterprises, which I have built into my price target analysis, I think that risk remains minimal, and at this price point, AKAM is an attractive buy.
